Product Design Case Study

Turning messy input into a record you can trust.

Daylog is an AI-assisted, calorie-first tracker — built for the people who keep starting calorie apps and quietly quitting them.

RoleProduct & UX Design
ScopeDiscovery → First Mockups
PlatformDesigned native for iOS & Android
The AI helps you log your day. The interface helps you understand it.
Daylog · capture · today · challenges
01 The problem

Logging is tedious. AI fixes that — and breaks trust.

People abandon calorie trackers because logging is too many steps: search, portion, meal, save, repeat. Chat-based AI removes that friction but introduces new problems — endless history, no control, hallucinated numbers, and no good way to review past days.

RAW INPUTchicken burrito and a coke
→
Chicken burrito600
Coca-Cola140
Total740

The real gap: no app lets you move between fast-and-approximate and precise-and-trusted as the moment demands.

02 Discovery

What the research said.

A scan of the market and the complaints users actually leave revealed a consistent story — and pointed straight at the opening.

FINDING 01

AI chat is table stakes

Describe-it or snap-a-photo logging is now the dominant pattern. The chat alone is no longer a differentiator — the field is crowded.

FINDING 02

Trust is the #1 failure

Every AI tracker gets the same complaint: wrong portions, missed ingredients, can't read a sandwich. Accuracy is where the category breaks.

FINDING 03

Consistency > precision

The nutrition evidence is clear — reliable trends matter more than perfect numbers. Staying engaged beats getting every calorie exactly right.

→ The edge isn't AI capture. It's trust and control.

03 Who it's for

One primary user — and three tensions to balance.

Primary

Maya

34 · busy · quit twice

Wants to log in seconds and trust the number without checking each item. Speed and accuracy, together.

Design for her first
Control

Daniel

42 · methodical

Doesn't trust the AI. Wants to enter food himself and see the math.

→ the manual, no-AI path
Depth

Priya

29 · fitness-led

Likes logging. Wants macros, trends, activity tied into net energy.

→ macros, calendar, challenges
Care

Sofia

26 · quit from stress

Tracking once tipped into anxiety. Needs a calm, non-judgmental tone.

→ the design conscience
THE CORE INSIGHT

Trust doesn't come from the AI being right. It comes from the AI being correctable.

A skeptic doesn't need a perfect tool — she needs one she can override in two taps. That reframes the AI's job: not to be flawless, but to be a cheap-to-fix first draft.

04 The spine

Three journeys that hold the product up.

Validated against Maya, these interlock into a loop — each one feeding the next.

WIN TRUST

First log

A skeptic types her first meal and gets a fast result she can sanity-check, item by item.

The make-or-break moment
WIN RETENTION

Repeat meal

The app learns her regulars and saves them — next time it's one tap, and exact.

The differentiator: the library
SURVIVE MISTAKES

AI got it wrong

A number looks off; she taps the item and fixes it in seconds. The fix teaches the library.

Trust through correctability
05 Key decisions

What the insight made true.

01

Two doors, one record

Chat for fast, messy capture; manual entry for precise control. Both write to the same structured day — never two separate apps.

02

Never an opaque total

Every number breaks down into tappable per-item lines. You can't trust — or fix — what you can't see.

03

One record, food and movement

The day shows calories in from food and out from activity, netted — with challenges to keep the habit alive.

04

Native on both platforms

iOS and Android were designed separately where users feel it — nav bars, controls, the add button, back behaviour. One brand, two native skins.

06 The screens

From empty state to a logged day.

The capture flow — an inviting empty state, a natural-language conversation that refines itself, and a clean logged result you can trust at a glance.

StartAn empty state that invites a first log — type, say, or snap.
iOS
CaptureNatural language in; the AI estimates, then tightens with specifics.
iOS
LoggedA clear breakdown with macros — checkable, then saved to today.
iOS
TodayThe hub — budget, macros, food, activity and the day's challenge.
iOS
ChallengesStreak-based goals that keep people coming back past week one.
iOS
Logged — AndroidThe same result, rebuilt to Material conventions.
Android
07 Two platforms, by design

The same screen, made to feel native on each.

Rather than ship one layout to both, each screen was designed twice — so it feels like it belongs on the device, not ported to it.

iOS
Android

Bottom navigation

iOS keeps a light, hairline tab bar. Android uses a filled bar with a pill highlight behind the active tab.

tab bar · vs · Material nav bar

System chrome

Status bar, signal and time styling follow each platform's own conventions rather than a single custom header.

SF cues · vs · Roboto cues

Controls & pickers

Editing uses the picker each user already knows — wheel selectors on iOS, steppers and fields on Android.

wheel · vs · stepper

Back & dismissal

Android respects the system back gesture; iOS leans on in-screen back and pull-to-dismiss sheets.

system back · vs · swipe down
08 What's next

Validate, then build out.

Light, sensitivity-aware user interviews to validate the proto-personas and core assumptions.
Synthesize gaps — quitter vs. current-user contrast, say/do gaps, tolerated friction.
Deepen habits & challenges — activity into net energy, streaks that stay kind.

DAYLOG · DESIGN CASE STUDY · DISCOVERY → FIRST MOCKUPS
